event: Eucharistic still life with chalice and host
symbols: golden chalice with host — the body and blood of Christ in the Eucharist
grapes and wheat sheaves — the raw materials of bread and wine transformed into the sacrament
lit candles — the light of Christ's presence in the Blessed Sacrament
abundant flowers — creation's offering of beauty before the Real Presence
butterflies — resurrection and the transformation of the soul through communion
description: A richly detailed still life centered on a golden chalice topped by a white circular host, set on a white cloth within a stone niche. Flanking the chalice are two silver candlesticks with lit candles and small vases of flowers. Above, two cornucopia-like arrangements overflow with grapes (red and white), wheat sheaves, and other fruits. Below the niche, a lavish garland of flowers — roses, tulips, carnations, morning glories — cascades across the lower portion. Small butterflies rest among the blooms. The palette is rich and dark with luminous highlights.
theological_description: This Eucharistic still life is a visual catechesis on the doctrine of the Real Presence. The golden chalice and white host at the center are the sacramental body and blood of Christ, the source and summit of Christian life (Catechism 1324). The grapes and wheat surrounding the chalice make explicit the connection between natural creation and sacramental transformation: bread and wine become the body and blood of Christ through the words of institution (Matthew 26:26-28). The lit candles signify Christ as the Light of the World, perpetually present in the tabernacle. The profusion of flowers expresses creation's response to the Eucharistic mystery — all beauty is an offering before the Real Presence. The butterflies, ancient symbols of resurrection, suggest the transformative power of communion: those who eat Christ's flesh and drink His blood have eternal life (John 6:54). This genre of Eucharistic still life emerged in the Counter-Reformation as a devotional aid for Eucharistic adoration.

scripture_refs.reason: The institution of the Eucharist — this is my body, this is my blood — the chalice and host depicted
Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink His blood you have no life — the theological basis of Eucharistic devotion
Paul's account of the Lord's Supper tradition, proclaiming the Lord's death until He comes
I am the true vine — the grapes as symbol of Christ and His life-giving sacramental presence
